questions.prompt: What's the common thread among the items that escalate to a senior across the customer lifecycle?
choices: Money, contract, or irreversible portal state.

choices.label: Anything that takes longer than 20 minutes to complete in the portal.
choices.rationale: Duration isn't the criterion. Some long tasks are tech work; some short clicks are senior decisions.

choices.label: Anything that involves opening a Huntress support ticket.
choices.rationale: Support tickets are a route, not the criterion. Most tech work doesn't involve support.

choices.label: Anything that requires the customer's CEO on a call.
choices.rationale: The criterion is the category of decision, not who happens to be on the call.

questions.explanation: Commercial, scope, and irreversible-state decisions stay with the senior. The escalation ceiling is the same line that opens and closes the customer-lifecycle course.

questions.prompt: What is the worst single failure mode in bulk agent rollout?
choices: Wrong-org-key enrolment, agents land in the wrong customer's tenant.

choices.label: 5% of endpoints failing to install on first push, requiring a second RMM job run to pick up stragglers.
choices.rationale: Normal and manageable; not the worst failure mode.

choices.label: Some endpoints taking 30 minutes to phone home.
choices.rationale: That's slow, not a failure at all.

choices.label: RMM job logs being slow to load.
choices.rationale: Operational annoyance, not a failure mode.

questions.explanation: Wrong-tenant enrolment is the worst class of failure. The installer was bundled with the wrong org key, so agents land under a different customer. The global Agents view filtered by enrolment time catches this in the first ten minutes.

questions.prompt: AD has 247 computer objects, RMM has 198, so 49 endpoints are in AD but not in RMM. What's the right response?

choices.label: Push the agent to all 49 to close the coverage gap.
choices.rationale: You'd be adding 49 unmanaged endpoints into the rollout without senior sign-off; deployment-strategy decision.

choices: Bump to a senior, surface the count, let them decide scope.

choices.label: Ignore them; they're not in the RMM, so they're not the MSP's concern and shouldn't be in the rollout.
choices.rationale: The MSP may or may not want them in scope; that's exactly the senior's call. Ignoring closes the question without a decision.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket asking what to do.
choices.rationale: Not a Huntress question; it's an MSP-internal deployment-strategy question.

questions.explanation: Deployment-strategy question. AD has 247, RMM has 198, so 49 are unmanaged. Surface the gap cleanly and let the senior decide scope.

questions.prompt: Order the six offboarding steps.
items: Pause new alert notifications for the customer's organisation.
Archive or export incidents per retention policy.
Raise the billing-closure ticket for the senior.
questions.explanation: Pause first so later steps don't fan alerts out. Identity before endpoint. Commercial wrap-up is the last, irreversible step the senior owns.

questions.prompt: "Uninstalling the agents is offboarding." Why is this a misconception?
choices: One of six steps; alone it leaves ITDR, SIEM, notifications, and billing still active.

choices.label: Uninstall is optional; ITDR disconnect alone is enough to fully close out a customer.
choices.rationale: Inverts the lesson. Both are required.

choices.label: Uninstall is the last step; everything before it is preparation.
choices.rationale: Uninstall is step 3, not last. Billing closure is last.

choices.label: Uninstall triggers automatic ITDR disconnect.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: Doing it alone leaves ITDR live, SIEM ingesting where applicable, notifications fanning out, and the org commercially open. Half-done offboardings are the most common state to inherit.

questions.prompt: You sign off the final checklist with the agent line as "ticked, with 4 of 198 resistant pending follow-up." What's the lesson's framing of this?
choices: The half-done state in disguise. Resolve to zero or leave the item un-ticked.

choices.label: Fine, 196/198 is functionally zero for sign-off purposes and the remainder can be mopped up later.
choices.rationale: Worst response. The four are still phoning home.

choices.label: Acceptable as long as the four are listed in a separate doc.
choices.rationale: Side-docs aren't substitutes for honest ticks on the primary checklist.

choices.label: Required, Huntress reporting expects a tick to be filed even if work is outstanding.
choices.rationale: Not a real reporting expectation.

questions.explanation: Either resolve to zero (or to a documented exception) or leave the item un-ticked. Don't pre-tick to look complete. The checklist's value is its honesty.

questions.prompt: Mid-offboarding. You paused notifications but haven't disconnected ITDR yet. The account manager pings: "Customer's IT manager just emailed asking why his inbox is suddenly quiet. Can we turn notifications back on so he isn't confused?"

choices.label: Turn notifications back on; the customer's IT manager has asked.
choices.rationale: The pause is there exactly because subsequent steps will generate signals; turning it back on now reverses the discipline.

choices: Keep the pause. The right response is a status update to the customer, not flipping notifications back on.

choices.label: Let the pause stay off and run the disconnect immediately; the inbox alerts from ITDR teardown won't be that bad.
choices.rationale: Bypasses the sequence's whole reason for being. Pause first means alerts don't fan out.

choices.label: Tell the account manager to send the IT manager a screenshot of the silent inbox to confirm it's intentional.
choices.rationale: Doesn't address the issue. The customer needs a status comms, not evidence of silence.

questions.explanation: The pause stays. Notifications were paused so the disconnect and uninstall steps don't fan alerts out. Customer perception is managed through the comms path, not by reversing the discipline.

questions.prompt: Which set of situations across the onboarding-and-offboarding course all escalate to a senior under the same ceiling?
choices: Skip GWS coverage; AD-not-in-RMM gap; keep OAuth artefact post-offboarding; delete the org.

choices.label: RMM job failing to run; portal showing fewer agents than the RMM after 10 minutes; trickling enrolment rate; resistant agents on the same site.
choices.rationale: All four are tech-work investigations, not commercial/scope/irreversible decisions.

choices.label: Setting up the organisation name per runbook; applying the partner branding template; pushing the RMM job during the documented install window; ticking the verify checklist.
choices.rationale: All four are documented runbook execution, explicitly in-scope tech work.

choices.label: Reading the analyst's recommendation; following the recommendation; closing an incident per the recommendation; documenting the close.
choices.rationale: All four are routine incident triage work; no commercial/scope/irreversible-state component.

questions.explanation: All four are commercial, scope, or irreversible-state decisions. Customer asks to skip GWS coverage (scope), AD-not-in-RMM gap (deployment strategy), keep OAuth artefact post-offboarding (irreversible state), delete the org (irreversible). The same boundary that opens onboarding closes offboarding.

questions.prompt: "Install succeeded" and "agent registered" differ how?
choices: Install puts binaries on disk and starts the service; registration is the first phone-home. They fail separately.

choices.label: They mean the same thing in different words; the portal uses both interchangeably depending on the view.
choices.rationale: Inverts the whole framing of agent diagnostics.

choices.label: Install is Huntress-side; registration is the RMM's job.
choices.rationale: Reverses the responsibilities.

choices.label: Install is for Windows; registration is for macOS and Linux.
choices.rationale: Not a platform split.

questions.explanation: Registration is the agent's first phone-home enrolling against the org key. The install-vs-registration split drives most agent-lifecycle diagnostics. They fail separately and need separate troubleshooting paths.

questions.prompt: What's the right-fix path for an AV-blocked install?
choices: Documented AV exclusions pushed via the AV management console.

choices.label: Temporarily disable AV for the install window, then re-enable once the agent registers.
choices.rationale: Removes coverage during install and trains the customer to disable AV when something gets in the way.

choices.label: Switch the customer to a different AV product.
choices.rationale: Wildly out of scope; deployment-strategy decision.

choices.label: Re-run the install five times until it gets through.
choices.rationale: Named anti-pattern. AV behaviour isn't random.

questions.explanation: AV and Huntress are designed to coexist. Documented exclusions pushed through the AV management console are the contract.

questions.prompt: Why is "bypass AppLocker via PowerShell as a one-off install" called out as the worst response to a policy block?
choices: Evading a security control the customer set up; the next upgrade hits the same block.

choices.label: PowerShell bypasses are technically impossible because AppLocker blocks all script hosts by default.
choices.rationale: They're sometimes possible; the objection is discipline, not capability.

choices.label: PowerShell triggers AV alerts that block the install.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's reason.

choices.label: Bypasses void Huntress's support contract.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

questions.explanation: Even if the bypass worked, the next upgrade fails the same way, and you've taught the runbook a bypass instead of the documented allow path. The allow path is the contract.

questions.prompt: Which signature points cleanly at blocked outbound versus another install-failure class?
choices: Service running, local log shows repeated connection failures; portal shows no agent.

choices.label: Binary quarantined by AV mid-install; service starts then dies within seconds on the endpoint.
choices.rationale: That's AV interference, a different failure class.

choices.label: Installer exits with "unsupported OS" message.
choices.rationale: That's an OS-support failure, a different class.

choices.label: AppLocker event under Microsoft-Windows-AppLocker.
choices.rationale: That's a policy block, a different class.

questions.explanation: The dormant-installed-not-registered fingerprint. Install worked; the agent's local log shows repeated DNS/TCP/TLS/HTTP failures against documented Huntress hostnames. Registration phone-home is being blocked outbound.

questions.prompt: Which set is the four causes of an offline agent?
choices: Powered off; service stopped; network blocked; uninstalled.

choices.label: AV interference; policy block; unsupported OS; missing dependency.
choices.rationale: Install-failure causes, not offline causes.

choices.label: Crashed; rebooted; uninstalled; replaced.
choices.rationale: Not the named enumeration.

choices.label: Pending; offline; archived; deleted.
choices.rationale: Portal states, not causes.

questions.explanation: The four causes drive the two-question diagnostic: is the endpoint online in the RMM, and is the service running on the endpoint.

questions.prompt: WS-CONTOSO-EXE-04 is online in the RMM and the Huntress service entry doesn't exist on the endpoint. What's the right move BEFORE re-pushing?
choices: Investigate why the agent was removed before re-pushing.

choices.label: Re-push immediately; uninstalled means re-install, and the sooner it's back the less telemetry you lose.
choices.rationale: Shotgun fix; re-installs on top of the same removal condition.

choices.label: Reboot the endpoint.
choices.rationale: Doesn't help with an uninstalled agent.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket.
choices.rationale: Premature; investigate locally first.

questions.explanation: Check whether AV update, user action, or deployment-tool cleanup removed it. Re-pushing on top of the same condition hits the same failure within hours.

questions.prompt: An agent won't upgrade. Which toolkit does the lesson say to diagnose with?
choices: The install-failure toolkit: AV-conflict, policy-block, and blocked-outbound.

choices.label: Always a Huntress-side regression; open a support ticket immediately and wait for a patch.
choices.rationale: Premature.

choices.label: Always a customer-side conscious decision; ignore.
choices.rationale: Inverts the lesson.

choices.label: Always rollback territory; bump to senior immediately.
choices.rationale: Rollback is for post-upgrade crashes, not won't-upgrade.

questions.explanation: Won't-upgrade is the install-failure toolkit applied to a different operation. AV, policy, and outbound blocks all reappear on upgrade attempts.

questions.prompt: Match the agent-move path to its use-case.
choices: Move-in-place for continuity; uninstall-and-re-install for clean break.

choices.label: Move-in-place for Windows; uninstall-and-re-install for macOS and Linux agents.
choices.rationale: Not a platform split.

choices.label: Move-in-place for free orgs; uninstall-and-re-install for paid orgs.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: Move-in-place for newer agents; uninstall-and-re-install for older agents.
choices.rationale: Not the discriminator.

questions.explanation: Move-in-place when telemetry continuity matters (active investigation, baseline period). Uninstall-and-re-install for clean handovers, degraded agent, or wrong-org-key fix. The runbook names which path applies to which scenario.

questions.prompt: What is the six-step spine of the standard EDR incident workflow?
choices: Claim, review, approve remediations, verify, close, document.

choices.label: Read, sandbox, decide, act, document, archive.
choices.rationale: Includes investigation work (sandbox) which is above the helpdesk ceiling.

choices.label: Approve, isolate, call, verify, close, escalate.
choices.rationale: Skips claim and review; isolate isn't part of every workflow.

choices.label: Triage, classify, recommend, action, follow up, close.
choices.rationale: Those are the SOC's pre-handoff steps, not the tech's.

questions.explanation: Claim, review, approve, verify, close, document. The same six steps on Low and High; tempo and paired actions shift with severity.

questions.prompt: The portal shows a remediation as "applied." Why isn't that the same as "verified"?
choices: Applied is command-ran; verified is end-state matches intent. Different checks.

choices.label: They're the same thing; the portal uses different words for the same state depending on the view.
choices.rationale: The lesson is explicit that they're different.

choices.label: Verified means the SOC analyst signed off; applied means the agent processed it.
choices.rationale: The SOC doesn't ratify verify. The tech does the endpoint-state check.

choices.label: Verified means the customer confirmed the fix worked; applied means the portal flipped state.
choices.rationale: Customer-feel isn't a verify input.

questions.explanation: Applied means the remediation command ran. Verified means the end state on the endpoint matches what the Recommendation aimed for. The tech owns the second check.

questions.prompt: Batch-approving autoruns remediations across multiple unrelated open incidents is:
choices: A common-mistake pattern. Each remediation is per-incident, per-host.

choices.label: The recommended pattern for fast queue clearance when you have a backlog of similar incidents.
choices.rationale: Inverts the rule.

choices.label: Allowed only on Low-severity incidents.
choices.rationale: Lows aren't carved out; each incident gets its own attention.

choices.label: Allowed only when all tickets are on the same customer.
choices.rationale: Same-customer batching still produces wrong-host errors.

questions.explanation: Produces the 'approved twelve at once and one was for a different host than I thought' failure. Each incident's recommendation is for its specific autoruns entry on its specific host.

questions.prompt: Huntress host isolation only works while the endpoint is on the customer's corporate network.
questions.explanation: The agent enforces the policy, not the customer's firewall. As long as the agent can reach Huntress, isolation applies wherever the endpoint is — home WiFi, hotel network, anywhere.

questions.prompt: Order the four moves of the plain-language isolation explanation to a customer.
items: Name the trigger: activity that needs immediate containment.
Name the action: we've put it into a protected isolation state.
Name the limits: you can still log in locally and use what's on the machine, but it can't reach the network.
Name the next step: we're working on it; I'll call you when it's ready to come out of isolation.
questions.explanation: Trigger, action, limits, next step. Avoids over- and under-claiming, tells the customer what still works, sets a callback expectation.

questions.prompt: Which sentence states the decision-tree spine for isolation correctly?
choices: Critical = isolate first; High = follow the Recommendation; Low = no isolation.

choices.label: Always isolate first, regardless of severity, since containment beats caution every time.
choices.rationale: Over-aggressive — Lows aren't an isolation surface.

choices.label: Always call the user first, regardless of severity.
choices.rationale: Under-aggressive on Criticals and isolation-recommended Highs; gives the attacker time.

choices.label: Isolate only when the customer's IT manager has signed off in writing.
choices.rationale: Inverts the model. The SOC's Recommendation drives isolation; customer sign-off isn't required for action.

questions.explanation: Severity drives the isolation choice through the SOC's Recommendation. Critical contains first; High follows the Recommendation; Low is not an isolation surface.

questions.prompt: "Frustrating customer, took forever to reach them" in an incident note is which named failure?
choices: Personal commentary. Tone-stripped facts only.

choices.label: Useful colour that helps the next tech understand customer context and communication style.
choices.rationale: Inverts the rule. Customer-relationship signals go in follow-ups with a destination, not in the incident note.

choices.label: Standard customer-relationship documentation.
choices.rationale: Personal commentary doesn't belong in the note; the right surface is a follow-up with a named recipient.

choices.label: Allowed as long as the senior approves the wording.
choices.rationale: Not the framing; the rule is tone-stripped facts in the note regardless.

questions.explanation: Tone-stripped facts. The incident note is for the next shift, the auditor, and future-you; commentary doesn't help any of them.

questions.prompt: Which combination correctly lists the four Critical markers?
choices: Ransomware canary trip; mass deletion; lateral movement signals; admin-credential abuse.

choices.label: Failed login; password spray; phishing email; reused credential.
choices.rationale: Identity-side signals, mostly Low-or-High-shape on their own. Don't match the Critical cluster.

choices.label: Slow agent enrolment; missing endpoint; stalled rollout; AV conflict.
choices.rationale: All onboarding-domain, not Critical-incident markers.

choices.label: Unsigned binary; new process tree; first-seen executable; rare hash.
choices.rationale: Process-detection signals the SOC weighs into a classification — markers of investigation, not of Critical itself.

questions.explanation: Canary trip, mass deletion, lateral movement, admin-credential abuse. The four markers cluster the most common Critical patterns.

questions.prompt: On a Critical, a slightly imperfect 30-second containment beats an exactly-right 5-minute one.
questions.explanation: Time matters more than completeness on Critical. The Low-and-High instinct of thoroughness-first inverts here — every extra second is endpoint-time the attacker keeps.

questions.prompt: The Critical-response rule, in three steps:
choices: Contain; escalate in parallel; do not investigate.

choices.label: Investigate; classify; contain.
choices.rationale: The opposite reflex. Investigation is above the line on Critical.

choices.label: Notify the customer; isolate; document.
choices.rationale: Wrong sequencing. Customer comms come after containment and after senior awareness.

choices.label: Wait for senior direction; contain only when instructed.
choices.rationale: The freeze failure mode. Containment doesn't wait.

questions.explanation: Contain, escalate in parallel, do not investigate. One motion across every Critical pattern.

questions.prompt: Senior arrives 5 minutes after the bump and asks: "Quick, what was on the endpoint?" You held the line and didn't investigate. What do you say?
choices: "I didn't look. I was waiting for you."

choices.label: "I read the Evidence section while waiting; here's what I saw."
choices.rationale: You stepped over the line. Forensic value of the endpoint is now in question.

choices.label: "I checked the customer's PSA history for related tickets while I waited."
choices.rationale: Wrong direction. The kind of "I'll be helpful" investigation that isn't help.

choices.label: "I started looking at running processes but didn't finish."
choices.rationale: Partial overstep is still overstep. Order-of-volatility costs apply to a partial process-tree query too.

questions.explanation: "I held the line" is the right starting answer. Whether the senior was testing or genuinely wants you to dig under direction, the line was held.

questions.prompt: Mid-shift you spot a High EDR incident on a customer's helpdesk workstation. Recommendation is approve autoruns remediation. Three minutes into your review, the Evidence shows the customer's domain-admin credential was used to remote-execute on three other endpoints in the last six hours. What sequence of actions is right?
choices: Recognise the Critical markers. Stop the High workflow. Isolate and bump the senior in parallel.

choices.label: Finish the High workflow as recommended since the SOC tagged it High; report the pattern observation at end-of-shift.
choices.rationale: Misses the framing — the High tag was what the SOC had when the report opened; the tech's Evidence read is the new contribution and the response model switches the moment the markers are clear.

choices.label: Re-grade the incident to Critical in the portal yourself; then act on Critical-shape response.
choices.rationale: Severity is the SOC's call. Bump rather than re-grade.

choices.label: Reply to the SOC asking for re-classification; wait for re-classification before isolating.
choices.rationale: Defensible to send in parallel, but containment doesn't wait for the tag. The pattern is the trigger.

questions.explanation: Pattern recognition is the tech's contribution. Contain + escalate in parallel + don't investigate; let the SOC's tag follow the action.
